items.q: Which item is removed first from a stack?
items.why: Students confuse stacks (LIFO - like a stack of plates) with queues (FIFO - like a checkout line). The 'stack of plates' analogy makes LIFO concrete.

items.q: Why must a recursive function have a base case?
items.correct: Without it, the function calls itself indefinitely, causing a stack overflow.
items.random_wrong: To make the function run faster.
items.why: Students treat the base case as optional ceremony. It's the TERMINATION CONDITION that stops recursion - each call adds a stack frame, and without termination the stack will overflow.

items.correct: The ability of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way.
items.misconception: Having multiple classes with the same name.
items.random_wrong: Variables that change type at runtime.
items.why: Students confuse polymorphism with naming. Polymorphism means 'many forms' - a single interface (method signature) behaving differently depending on the object's actual class.

items.q: Which data type stores True or False?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Boolean. “Integer” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Integer. “Real” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many possible values can a Boolean variable hold?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 values. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
